identity_summary: The internet knows you as 'testuser' across 47 platforms including GitHub, Reddit, and Twitter. Your address at 123 Main St and phone number appear on 4 data broker sites. Credentials from 3 breaches (LinkedIn 2012, Adobe 2013, Dropbox 2012) are in circulation, including a SHA1 password hash.

physical_data: Address: 123 Main St, San Francisco CA (Spokeo, Whitepages)
credentials_exposed: Password hash (SHA1) from LinkedIn breach, May 2016
Password hash (MD5) from Adobe breach, October 2013
google_footprint: 12 Google Maps reviews (restaurants in San Francisco)

top_risks: Password hash from LinkedIn 2016 breach is in active circulation — change any reused passwords immediately
Physical address and phone number appear on 4 broker sites — accessible to anyone
Username 'testuser' links accounts across 47 platforms enabling full identity correlation
Google Maps reviews reveal frequent locations including likely home neighborhood
Relatives' names and associations are publicly listed on broker sites

identity_fraud_prevention: Get an IRS Identity Protection PIN at irs.gov/identity-theft-fraud-scams/get-an-identity-protection-pin — prevents fraudulent tax filings in your name. Free, renews annually.
Lock your SSN in E-Verify at myeverify.uscis.gov — prevents your SSN from being used in employment eligibility checks.
Enroll in USPS Informed Delivery at informeddelivery.usps.com — get email previews of your mail to detect redirection fraud.
credit_freeze: Freeze your credit at all bureaus: Equifax (equifax.com/freeze), Experian (experian.com/freeze), TransUnion (transunion.com/freeze), Innovis (innovis.com/freeze). Also freeze: ChexSystems (chexsystems.com, protects bank accounts), LexisNexis Risk Solutions (optout.lexisnexis.com, used in background checks and insurance).
sim_swap_hardening: Contact your mobile carrier (AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile) and add a verbal passcode or port-freeze to prevent SIM-swap attacks.
Remove your phone number from social media account recovery on Facebook, Twitter/X, and Google — replace with an authenticator app.
change_passwords: Change passwords for all breached accounts: LinkedIn (2016), Adobe (2013), Dropbox (2012). Use a unique password for each — consider 1Password or Bitwarden.
enable_2fa: Enable 2FA on: GitHub, Twitter, Reddit — use an authenticator app (Google Authenticator, Authy) rather than SMS.
account_hygiene: Revoke unused OAuth app access on: Google (myaccount.google.com/permissions), Facebook, Twitter/X — remove any apps you no longer use.
Audit active sessions: check Google (myaccount.google.com/device-activity), Apple ID, and Microsoft for unrecognized devices.
Delete unused accounts — use justdeleteme.xyz to find step-by-step deletion instructions.
account_reviews: Review privacy/visibility settings on: GitHub, Twitter, Reddit, Instagram — remove phone number from public view, set profile indexing to off where possible.
ccpa_removals: CCPA deletion request to LinkedIn at https://www.linkedin.com/help/linkedin/answer/a1340440
CCPA deletion request to Adobe at https://www.adobe.com/privacy/privacy-contact.html or privacy@adobe.com
broker_optouts: Use EasyOptOuts (easyoptouts.com, ~$20/year) to automate removal from Spokeo, Whitepages, BeenVerified, and 100+ brokers — profiles re-populate every 90 days so an ongoing subscription is recommended.
Manual opt-out: Spokeo at spokeo.com/optout — has your address and phone
monitoring: Sign up for free breach monitoring at haveibeenpwned.com with your email address to get notified of future breaches immediately.
Set Google Alerts for your full name, phone number, and home address at google.com/alerts.
Re-run data broker opt-outs every 90 days — profiles re-populate from public records.
Review active OAuth app permissions quarterly.
no_action_available: LinkedIn (2016), Adobe (2013), Dropbox (2012) — breach archives held by third parties, no removal possible. Monitor with HIBP for future breaches.

findings_context.name: LinkedIn
findings_context.what_it_is: Professional networking platform used for career and business connections.
findings_context.why_it_matters: Your 2016 breach data (email + password hash) is in active circulation; your current profile is also publicly indexed.

findings_context.name: Adobe
findings_context.what_it_is: Creative software company whose 2013 breach exposed 153M accounts.
findings_context.why_it_matters: Your email and MD5-encrypted password from the 2013 breach are searchable in breach databases.

findings_context.name: Spokeo
findings_context.what_it_is: People-search data broker aggregating public records and social media data.
findings_context.why_it_matters: Your home address, phone number, and relatives are listed and accessible to anyone.

findings_context.name: Whitepages
findings_context.what_it_is: People-search data broker with address, phone, and background check data.
findings_context.why_it_matters: Your address and phone appear in search results, enabling stalking or targeted phishing.

findings_context.name: Dropbox
findings_context.what_it_is: Cloud storage service that suffered a 2012 breach exposing 68M accounts.
findings_context.why_it_matters: Breach archive data is in third-party databases — cannot be removed.
